content.text: Hub-and-Spoke vs Federated Claude Operating Models

When an organization has moved beyond early-stage Claude adoption and needs to scale across multiple business units, it faces a fundamental operating model choice: should Claude delivery be coordinated through a central hub with spokes extending to each business unit, or should each business unit operate autonomously within a set of shared standards? These two approaches — hub-and-spoke and fully federated — represent the main paths for scaling enterprise Claude programs.

This document examines each model's mechanics, trade-offs, transition criteria, and hybrid variations, giving organizations a framework for selecting and evolving the right structure for their context.
The Core Distinction
Both models assume a central function (the CoE or a thin standards group), but differ in how much authority, capability, and delivery responsibility lives at the center versus in business units:

attrs.alt: Hub-and-Spoke vs Federated Claude operating models

content.text: Hub-and-Spoke:

the central CoE (hub) is the primary delivery entity. Business unit champions (spokes) act as local liaisons and enablers, but they route requests back to the hub for fulfillment, technical review, and governance approval. The hub holds the expertise and the authority.
Fully Federated:

business units own their Claude delivery end-to-end. A thin central function maintains shared standards, tooling, and policy, but does not fulfill requests — BU-embedded AI engineers handle design, implementation, governance, and evaluation. The center sets guardrails, not the agenda.

Hub-and-spoke maximizes consistency and control. Federated maximizes speed and local autonomy. Neither is inherently superior — the right choice depends on organizational maturity, risk tolerance, and adoption stage.
Hub-and-Spoke Model in Depth
How It Works
In the hub-and-spoke model:
A business unit (spoke) identifies a Claude use case and submits a request through the service catalog
The central CoE (hub) reviews the request, classifies risk, and assigns a CoE specialist
CoE specialists fulfill the service — delivering training, reviewing architecture, running risk assessment, or facilitating a discovery workshop
The BU champion coordinates locally, gathering requirements and ensuring the team engages with the CoE
After delivery, the CoE tracks quality metrics, SLA compliance, and requests feedback

The spoke (champion) role in this model is primarily relational and coordinative, not technical. Champions help the CoE understand BU context and help BU teams engage with CoE resources — but they do not own delivery.
When Hub-and-Spoke Works Best
Early adoption phase (first 12–24 months) when governance standards are still being established
Regulated industries where consistent policy application across all BUs is non-negotiable
Organizations where BUs lack the AI engineering talent to operate independently
Companies where executive sponsors want visible, centralized control over Claude investment
Organizations with a small number of Claude use cases that do not yet justify distributed capability
Hub-and-Spoke Failure Modes
Hub bottleneck:
as use case volume grows, the central CoE cannot keep up with demand. SLAs stretch; BUs get frustrated; shadow programs emerge.
Spoke passivity:
champions with no delivery responsibility become message-relayers rather than capability builders. They lose credibility with their teams.
Hub insularity:
CoE specialists who never embed in BUs develop generic solutions that don't fit domain-specific contexts well.
Governance theater:
high-volume, standardized requests get the same governance overhead as novel, high-risk ones — the process doesn't scale to volume.
Bottleneck Signal
Meaning
Remediation
SLA miss rate > 15% for 2+ months
Hub capacity is saturated
Either grow hub headcount or begin spoke autonomy expansion
Out-of-catalog request rate > 20%
Spokes are building independently anyway
Federate ownership of the most common out-of-catalog categories
Champion engagement declining
Champions feel irrelevant because they don't own delivery
Give champions Tier 2 decision rights and delivery authority
BU bypass rate rising
Teams route around the CoE directly
Reduce governance overhead for low-risk requests; push Tier 1 to self-service
Federated Model in Depth
How It Works
In the fully federated model:
Each business unit has one or more AI engineers with Claude expertise embedded in the team
BU teams design, build, and operate their own Claude integrations
A thin central function (sometimes called a 'platform team' or 'guild') maintains shared APIs, libraries, prompt standards, security policies, and evaluation frameworks
BUs pull from central resources as needed but do not submit requests to a central queue
The guild meets periodically to synchronize patterns, surface cross-BU learnings, and ratify standard changes
The central function in a federated model is a technical authority, not a delivery engine. It produces shared infrastructure and policy, not services.
When Federated Works Best
Mature organizations (24+ months into Claude adoption) with established governance norms
Organizations where Claude is a core product capability, not just a productivity aid
Large enterprises with 30+ active use cases where central delivery is genuinely impossible at the needed velocity
Companies with strong AI engineering talent distributed across BUs
Organizations that compete on AI delivery speed where centralized bottlenecks directly impact business outcomes
Federated Model Failure Modes
Standards drift:
BUs develop incompatible prompt patterns, different security assumptions, or conflicting model configurations. The organization fragments into isolated silos.
Redundant investment:
without coordination, multiple BUs build similar capabilities independently — different contract review tools, different data extraction prompts, different evaluation frameworks for similar tasks.
Compliance gaps:
BUs that own their own governance may miss regulatory requirements or make inconsistent risk decisions that create enterprise-level liability.
Inequitable access:
BUs with strong AI engineering talent pull ahead; BUs without it fall further behind. The organization bifurcates into haves and have-nots.

# Guild coordination mechanism — minimal structure to prevent drift

Guild meeting cadence: biweekly (60 min)
Required attendees: Lead AI Engineer from each BU, CoE standards lead
Agenda template:
  1. Pattern sharing (15 min): one BU presents a pattern they built this sprint
  2. Standards updates (10 min): any changes to central tooling, policy, or API
  3. Cross-BU opportunities (15 min): where are multiple BUs solving similar problems?
  4. Risk / compliance radar (10 min): any compliance signals the guild should know about
  5. Open discussion (10 min)

Guild artifacts:
  - Shared prompt library: vetted, cross-BU prompts that any team can use
  - Integration cookbook: patterns and anti-patterns for common Claude integrations
  - Decision log: significant architectural decisions and rationale
  - Risk radar: emerging compliance or safety signals relevant to all BUs
Hybrid Approaches
In practice, most large organizations operate a hybrid that combines elements of both models. Common hybrids:
Tiered Hub-and-Spoke

The hub retains responsibility for high-risk and novel use cases (Tier 3+) but delegates routine, low-risk delivery to BU champions (Tier 1–2). Champions have full delivery authority within defined guardrails — they don't need hub approval for standard use cases. This is the most common transition state for hub-and-spoke organizations beginning to federate.
Use Case Tier
Hub Role
Spoke Role
Tier 1 (low risk, standard)
Sets standards; available for questions
Full ownership — design, build, approve, operate
Tier 2 (medium risk)
Reviews and approves production readiness
Designs, builds; escalates to hub for approval
Tier 3+ (high risk, novel)
Leads delivery, owns approval
Provides domain context; implements under hub direction
Platform-Federated

The central function operates as a platform team, not a CoE. It builds and operates shared infrastructure (APIs, evaluation tooling, prompt management systems, observability) that BU teams consume as self-service. BU teams are autonomous for use case design and delivery but must use the central platform's tooling. This enforces technical consistency without requiring approval workflows.
What the platform owns:
Claude API gateway with access controls and usage metering; shared observability (logging, alerting, cost dashboards); prompt versioning and approval tooling; evaluation harnesses.
What BUs own:
use case design; prompt development within the platform's tooling; evaluation criteria; production deployment within platform guardrails.
Governance mechanism:
platform enforces standards via technical controls, not approval processes — if a prompt doesn't pass the automated eval gate, it can't be deployed, regardless of who built it.
Choosing and Transitioning Between Models
The right model depends on three factors: adoption maturity, risk profile, and engineering talent distribution. Use this decision framework:

# Operating model selection decision tree

Is this the first 18 months of Claude adoption?
  → Yes: Start with Hub-and-Spoke (centralized)

Is the organization in a heavily regulated industry (finance, healthcare)?
  → Yes: Hub-and-Spoke until governance frameworks are mature; then Tiered Hub-and-Spoke
  → No: consider federated sooner if talent exists

Does the org have 20+ active Claude use cases AND BU AI engineers?
  → Yes: transition to Federated or Tiered Hub-and-Spoke
  → No: stay Hub-and-Spoke; invest in BU champion capability first

Is Claude a core product capability (shipped to customers)?
  → Yes: Federated or Platform-Federated is likely necessary for delivery speed
  → No: Hub-and-Spoke or Tiered Hub-and-Spoke sufficient

Are SLA misses consistently above 15% for 3+ months?
  → Yes: Hub-and-Spoke is not scaling — transition to federated

Are BUs building independent, incompatible Claude solutions despite the hub?
  → Yes: Hub-and-Spoke has already informally federated — formalize it with clear standards
Managing the Hub-to-Federated Transition
The transition from hub-and-spoke to federated should be incremental, not a wholesale reorganization. A phased approach:
Phase 1 — Tiered Hub-and-Spoke (3–6 months):
give BU champions Tier 1 and Tier 2 decision rights. The hub focuses on Tier 3+ delivery and standards. Monitor for quality consistency before expanding.
Phase 2 — Pilot Federation (3–6 months):
identify 1–2 BUs with strong AI engineering talent and grant them full delivery authority for a defined use case category. Hub provides standards; BU owns the rest. Evaluate success before expanding.
Phase 3 — Full Federation (6–12 months):
expand federated model to all BUs with sufficient talent. Restructure the hub into a platform/standards function. Establish guild for coordination.
Measuring Model Effectiveness
Regardless of which model an organization runs, it should measure whether the model is achieving its goals. The right metrics differ by model:
Metric
Hub-and-Spoke Target
Federated Target
Why It Differs
SLA response compliance
N/A (no centralized SLA)
Federated BUs don't submit to a hub queue
Time-to-first-deployment
60–90 days
14–30 days
Federated teams move faster without approval queues
Cross-BU policy consistency
High (hub enforces uniformly)
Moderate-High (guild enforces via standards + tooling)
Federated model has more natural variation
BU AI engineer headcount
Not tracked centrally
Critical growth metric
Federated model depends on distributed talent
Out-of-catalog request rate
< 15% (hub catches gaps)
Tracked via guild, not a service catalog
Federated orgs don't use the same catalog model
Cost per use case deployed
Higher (hub overhead)
Lower (BU efficiency)
Direct delivery is more efficient at scale

One metric applies universally: time-to-first-value for a new team. Both models should be able to get a new team to their first production Claude use case within 90 days. If either model is taking longer, the onboarding path has friction that needs to be removed.
Governance Without a Central Gatekeeper
One of the most common concerns about federated models is governance: if each BU makes its own decisions, how does the organization maintain consistent risk management and policy compliance?
The answer is that governance mechanisms shift from process-based (approval queues) to standards-based (technical controls and clear policies):
Policy as code:

the central standards function publishes machine-readable policy rules that BU tooling automatically checks. A prompt that routes sensitive data to an unapproved model fails the policy check before deployment — no human review needed.
Automated risk classification:
a shared risk rubric is implemented as a form or tool that any BU engineer can run on their use case. Output is a risk tier that determines whether they can self-approve or must escalate.
Tiered accountability:
BU teams accept accountability for Tier 1 and Tier 2 decisions by self-certifying compliance. The central function audits a sample quarterly rather than reviewing everything upfront.
Incident-triggered review:
instead of pre-approving all use cases, the governance model reviews all incidents and near-misses. This is more efficient and focuses review effort where risk has actually materialized.

# Example: policy-as-code rule (enforced by central Claude API gateway)
policy_rules:
  - id: no-customer-pii-without-approval
    description: Customer PII cannot be sent to Claude without explicit risk approval
    trigger:
      model: claude-*
      prompt_contains_pii: true
      risk_approval: absent
    action: block
    error_message: >
      This request includes customer PII but no risk approval token.
      Submit a risk assessment at coe.internal/risk-assessment before
      deploying use cases that process customer data.

  - id: require-system-prompt
    description: All production deployments must include a system prompt
    trigger:
      environment: production
      system_prompt: null
    action: block
    error_message: >
      Production Claude requests must include a system prompt.
      See prompt standards at coe.internal/prompt-standards.

  - id: high-risk-model-restriction
    description: Claude Opus requires Tier 3 approval for production use
    trigger:
      model: claude-opus-*
      environment: production
      tier_3_approval: absent
    action: block
Cultural Prerequisites for Each Model
Operating model choice is not only a structural decision — it also depends on organizational culture. Each model requires a different cultural baseline to function:
Hub-and-Spoke Culture Prerequisites
Trust in central expertise:
BUs must believe the CoE's recommendations are worth waiting for, or they'll bypass the hub.
Patience with process:
teams must accept that governance review takes time and is worth it for risk management.
Executive mandate:
without top-down mandate to route through the CoE, BUs with strong engineers will go around it.
Federated Culture Prerequisites
Standards discipline:
BU engineers must follow shared standards even when they don't see the immediate benefit — non-compliance destroys the model's consistency advantage.
Community orientation:
BU engineers must contribute to the guild (sharing patterns, attending syncs, updating shared libraries) rather than free-riding on others' contributions.
Self-imposed accountability:
without central oversight, BU teams must be willing to run their own risk assessments honestly — not just check boxes to get to deployment faster.
Tolerance for inconsistency:
federated models will have more variation than hub-and-spoke. Leaders must accept this as the price of speed, rather than mandating uniformity that the model cannot deliver.
The hardest part of the hub-to-federated transition is not the structure — it's getting BU teams to internalize governance rather than expecting the hub to impose it.
Role Continuity Across Both Models
Certain roles exist in both models but play different parts:
Role
In Hub-and-Spoke
In Federated
CoE Lead / Platform Lead
Manages central delivery team; accountable for SLAs
Maintains standards and tooling; facilitates guild; no service delivery
AI Engineer
Works in the hub; fulfills requests across BUs
Embedded in a specific BU; owns local delivery end-to-end
Governance Analyst
Reviews every production use case before approval
Publishes risk rubric and policy; audits sample quarterly
BU Champion
Coordinates with hub; local liaison; limited delivery authority
May become AI Engineering Lead; owns all local decisions within standards
Executive Sponsor
Signs off on high-risk decisions; holds CoE budget
Holds platform/standards budget; delegates BU delivery authority
Summary

Hub-and-spoke and federated are complementary stages on the same adoption journey, not competing philosophies. Most organizations start hub-and-spoke, evolve toward federation as maturity grows, and ultimately land in a platform-federated hybrid where the center provides infrastructure and the edges own delivery.
Hub-and-spoke maximizes consistency and governance control — ideal for early adoption and regulated industries
Fully federated maximizes speed and local autonomy — requires mature governance norms and distributed AI talent
Tiered hub-and-spoke is the natural transition state: spokes gain Tier 1–2 autonomy while hub retains Tier 3+ ownership
Platform-federated enforces standards via technical controls rather than approval processes — the most scalable governance model
Transition signals: SLA miss rate >15%, BU bypass behavior, champion disengagement, and shadow Claude programs all indicate the hub-and-spoke model is too constraining
Guild coordination is the essential mechanism for federated models — without it, standards drift and redundant investment become inevitable
Choose the model that fits current maturity, not the model that fits aspirational maturity — premature federation before governance is established accelerates risk, not value
